title: SEGMENT 16 — CRISPR for metabolic engineering: knock out, knock down, redirect

lines.text: SEGMENT 16 — CRISPR for metabolic engineering: knock out, knock down, redirect

CRISPR — the gene-editing technology developed from bacterial immunity systems and adapted for use in eukaryotic cells by Jennifer Doudna, Emmanuelle Charpentier, Feng Zhang, George Church, and others around 2012-2013 — has been transformative for many areas of biology, and metabolic engineering is one of them.
We are not going to do a full primer on CRISPR mechanism here; that lives in a separate set of material.
The metabolic engineering perspective is what specifically the CRISPR tools have changed.
The first thing CRISPR changed was the ease of gene knockout in workhorse strains.

Before CRISPR, knocking out a gene in E. coli involved building a knockout cassette with antibiotic markers and homology arms, transforming it in, selecting recombinants, often curing the marker afterward — a multi-week process per gene.
With CRISPR-Cas9 plus a homologous repair template, the same operation can be done in days, often without leaving any selection marker behind.

You design the guide RNA to target the gene you want to knock out, you co-deliver a repair template with the desired modification, and Cas9 makes the cut, the cell repairs using your template, and you have your edit.
The cost per knockout has dropped dramatically.
The number of genes you can routinely manipulate per project has gone up.
In yeast, CRISPR has been even more transformative.

Yeast has very efficient homologous recombination — it was actually the easier-to-edit organism for many years — but CRISPR has made the cuts more targeted, the editing more efficient at hard-to-reach loci, and has enabled multiplexed editing of many sites at once by simply expressing multiple guide RNAs from a single construct.
The second thing CRISPR enabled is gene knockdown without knockout — the use of catalytically inactive Cas9 (dCas9) to block transcription at a target locus without actually cutting the DNA.
This technique, called CRISPRi (CRISPR interference), lets you reduce the expression of a gene to a tunable level rather than eliminating it entirely.
For metabolic engineering, this is enormously useful.

Many essential genes that you would want to downregulate to push flux through a heterologous pathway — for example, squalene synthase in yeast, which competes with the artemisinin pathway for FPP — cannot be knocked out entirely because the cell needs them to live.
With CRISPRi, you can dial them down to a tunable level, finding the sweet spot between cell viability and product titer.
The flip side, CRISPRa (CRISPR activation), uses dCas9 fused to a transcriptional activator to boost expression of target genes.
Together, CRISPRi and CRISPRa give you a tunable dial for any gene in the genome.
The third thing CRISPR enabled is library-scale screens for metabolic phenotypes.

You can build a CRISPR guide library targeting every gene in the genome — or a focused library targeting a particular set of pathways — and apply it to your engineered strain, then screen for cells with improved product titers.
The cells with knockouts or knockdowns that boost product accumulate, and you sequence them to identify which gene perturbations were beneficial.
This is a way of doing rational FBA-style strain design and exhaustive genome-wide screening in parallel, which often turns up improvements that the model did not predict.
The analogy I want you to carry: CRISPR for metabolic engineering is the high-throughput multi-locus editing scalpel.
Knock out competing pathways.
Knock down essential bottlenecks to a tunable level.
Activate underexpressed enzymes.
Run genome-wide library screens for productivity.
The cost and difficulty of every kind of genome manipulation has dropped, and the scale at which you can push flux into a target has gone up.
CRISPR sits alongside MAGE and the other recombineering tools we discussed in Segment 9.
They are complementary, not redundant.
MAGE is particularly good for simultaneously introducing many small modifications, often single base pair changes, across many loci.
CRISPR is better for precise, single-locus editing and for tunable transcriptional control.
Modern strain engineering campaigns use both, choosing the tool to fit the manipulation.

The combined effect of all these throughput-increasing tools — MAGE, REXER, CRISPR, MoClo, DNA synthesis — is that the cost of running a strain engineering campaign has dropped roughly an order of magnitude per decade for the last two decades.
What used to take a postdoc five years now takes a small team a year.
What used to require a dedicated lab now happens routinely at platform companies like Ginkgo.
The discipline is, in throughput terms, where electronics was somewhere in the 1980s — past the era of one-off custom designs, into the era of design tools and platform fabrication.
That is what enables the alt-protein wave, the plant natural product wave, the sustainable chemicals wave — all of them depend on engineering throughput that simply did not exist twenty years ago.
Now let us look at the layer that is starting to sit on top of all of this — the AI-driven design of metabolic pathways and enzymes.
